---
title: "TerrorMappingWriteup"
author: "Alex Rizzo-Banks"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: 
  html_document:
    code_folding: hide
    number_sections: TRUE
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: yes
  pdf_document:
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: '3'    
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r Read-In}
TerrorData = read.csv(file = "~/GW_ClassStuff/Data_Science_exploration/TerrorDB/TerrorData.csv",)
#"eventid" is structured according to date and then sequential ID of the incident (in case of multiple days), so: YYYYMMDD0001,0002,0003, etc
GTD1 = subset(TerrorData, TerrorData$eventid>200109210000)
#September 21st 2001 is the day after George Bush officially declared the U.S. War on Terror. We'll look at attacks since he said that.
library(dplyr)
GTDalex <- GTD1 %>% select(-c(approxdate,
                           region,
                           latitude, longitude,
                           specificity,
                           alternative,
                           attacktype1, attacktype2, attacktype3,         targtype1, targsubtype1, targtype2, targsubtype2, targtype3, targsubtype3,
                           natlty1, natlty2, natlty3, 
                           gname3, gsubname3, guncertain3, 
                           claimmode, 
                           claim2, claimmode2, claimmode2_txt, 
                           claim3, claimmode3, claimmode3_txt, 
                           weaptype1, weaptype2, weapsubtype1, weapsubtype2,
                  weaptype3, weaptype3_txt, weapsubtype3, weapsubtype3_txt,
                  weaptype4, weaptype4_txt, weapsubtype4, weapsubtype4_txt,
                  nkillus, nwoundus, 
                  propextent, 
          nhostkidus, nhours, ndays, divert, kidhijcountry, hostkidoutcome,
      ransom, ransomamt, ransomamtus, ransompaid, ransompaidus, ransomnote,
                            scite1, scite2, scite3, 
                            INT_ANY))
#We have removed 60 variables to narrow down the dataset for the scope of this class. Information on what was removed will be added later.
colnames(GTDalex) = c(
  "Chronological ID", "Year", "Month", "Day",
  "Attack ran multiple days?", "Date of resolution", 
  "Country code", "Country", "Region", "Province or state", "City", "Attack occurred within city limits?", "Description of location", 
  
  "Description of attack", "Attack meets Criteria 1?", "Attack meets Criteria 2?", "Attack meets Criteria 3?", "Terror label debated?", "Alternate label", "Multiple attacks?", 
  
  "Attack successful?", "Attackers commit suicide?", "Primary mode of attack", "Secondary mode of attack", "Tertiary mode of attack", 
  
  "Target primary type", 
  "Target secondary type", 
  "Targeted entity", 
  "Description of target", 
  "Nationality of target", 
  
  "2nd target primary type",
  "2nd target secondary type", 
  "2nd targeted entity", 
  "Description of 2nd target",
  "Nationality of 2nd target",
  
  "3rd target primary type",
  "3rd target secondary type", 
  "3rd targeted entity", 
  "Description of 3rd target",
  "Nationality of 3rd target",
  
  "Terror group name", "Terror group subname", "2nd terror group name", "2nd terror group subname", "Motive behind attack", "Terror group unconfirmed", "2nd terror group unconfirmed", 
  "Attack by single individual?", "Number of attackers", "Number of attackers captured",
  
  "Group claimed responsibility for attack?", "Mode of claim", "Competing claims?",
  
  "Primary weapon type", "Primary weapon subtype", "Secondary weapon type", "Secondary weapon subtype", "Description of weapon",
  
  "Total number of casualties", "Number of attackers killed", "Total number of wounded", "Number of attackers wounded", 
  "Damage. to property present?", "Level of property damage", "Total value of property damage", "Note on property damage",
  
  "Kidnapping occurred?", "Number kidnapped", "Outcome of kidnapping", "Number of hostages released",
  
  "Misc. notes", "Database used", 
  "Attacker nationality different from country?", "Attacker nationality different from target's?", "Target's nationality different from country?",
  
  "IDs of related attacks"
  )
library(ezids)
library(ggplot2)
#str(GTDalex)
GTD0208 = subset(GTDalex, GTDalex$`Chronological ID`>200201010000 & GTDalex$`Chronological ID`<200812310000)

GTD2014= subset(GTDalex, GTDalex$Year=="2014")

ggplot(GTDalex) + geom_bar(mapping = aes(GTDalex$Year))

ggplot(TerrorData) + geom_bar(mapping = aes(TerrorData$iyear))
```



# How does multiple attacks, weapon type, attack type, month of attack, or region of attack influence whether a terrorist attack causes property damage in 2019?

## Author of this section: Aspen Saplan

## Variable Key:

#### Property:

0 = No

1 = Yes

#### AttackType1:

1 = ASSASSINATION

2 = ARMED ASSAULT

3 = BOMBING/EXPLOSION

4 = HIJACKING

5 = HOSTAGE TAKING (BARRICADE INCIDENT)

6 = HOSTAGE TAKING (KIDNAPPING)

7 = FACILITY / INFRASTRUCTURE ATTACK

8 = UNARMED ASSAULT

9 = UNKNOWN

#### WeaponType1:

1 = Biological

2 = Chemical

3 = Radiological

4 = Nuclear

5 = Firearms

6 = Explosives

7 = Fake Weapons

8 = Incendiary

9 = Melee

10 = Vehicle

11 = Sabotage Equipment

12 = Other

13 =  Unknown

#### Multiple:

0 = No

1 = Yes

#### Month:

1 = Jan

2 = Feb

3 = Mar

4 = April

5 = May

6 = June

7 = July

8 = Aug

9 = Sep

10 = Oct

11 = Nov

12 = Dec

#### Region:

1 = North America

2 = Central America & Caribbean

3 = South America

4 = East Asia

5 = Southeast Asia

6 = South Asia

7 = Central Asia

8 = Western Europe

9 = Eastern Europe

10 = Middle East & North Africa

11 = Sub-Saharan Africa

12 = Australasia & Oceania


## Set up of terror2019 dataset:

Multiple attacks variable represents if the record was one singular terrorist attack or if there were multiple combined that represent one terrorist attack.  The weapon type variable represents what type of weapon was used to perform the terrorist attack.  The month of attack variable represents what month the terrorist attack occurred in.  The region variable represents the region that the terrorist attack took place in.  The property variable is the dependent variable that will be examined and represents whether there was property damage or there was no property damage caused by this terrorist attack.  All of these variables are of the type categorical variable, so the first thing that must be done is as factoring them so that r studio can represent them correctly.  

```{r,echo=TRUE}
terror2019 = read.csv("TerrorData-2019.csv")
# If other datasets are used and include more than years 2019
# terror2019 = terror2019[terror2019$iyear == 2019, ]
terror2019 = terror2019[, c("property", "multiple", "weaptype1", "attacktype1", "imonth", "region")]
terror2019 = terror2019[terror2019$property != -9 & !is.na(terror2019$property),]
terror2019$property = as.factor(terror2019$property)
terror2019$multiple = as.factor(terror2019$multiple)
terror2019$weaptype1 = as.factor(terror2019$weaptype1)
terror2019$attacktype1 = as.factor(terror2019$attacktype1)
terror2019$imonth= as.factor(terror2019$imonth)
terror2019$region = as.factor(terror2019$region)
str(terror2019)
```
## Balance Check:

The next part of exploratory data analysis that is looked at is the ratio of zeros and ones in the property variable in order to know if this variable is balanced.  Which as seen this variable is about fifty four percent no property damage and forty six percent yes property damage.  Which is a pretty good balance of both yes and no values indicating that this is a good dependent variable choice for logistic regression modelling.  

Number of records in 2019 with either property damage or no property damage:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
total = nrow(terror2019)
total
```

Number of records in 2019 without property damage:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
npd = sum(terror2019$property == 0)
npd
```

Number of records in 2019 with property damage:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
pd = sum(terror2019$property == 1)
pd
```

Percentage of records without property damage:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
(npd/total)
```

Percentage of records with property damage:
```{r,echo=TRUE}
(pd/total)
```

## EDA using Bar Charts:

As seen below are all the bar charts that represent the number of property damage compared to each potential covariate.  The red bars represent no property damage, while the purple bars represent yes property damage.

For multiple terrorist attacks, no property damage is favored for single terrorist attacks, while yes property damage is favored for multiple terrorist attacks.  Although, the single terrorist attack overall has more values then multiple terrorist attacks.  

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = multiple, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Multiple Attacks",
    x = "Multiple",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

The weapon type variable shows a mix of having more yes property damage and no property damage.  What is interesting though is the insight on which weapons are used more often in 2019 for a terrorist attack.  Overall, weapon five, six, eight, and thirteen make up the majority of weapon types used compared to all the rest.  Some weapon types did not even occur in 2019 such as weapon three and four.  

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = weaptype1, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Weapon Type",
    x = "Weapon Type",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

The attack type variable shows similar results to the weapon type variable in the sense that some attacks caused more property damage then not.  While some attacks caused more no property damage than actual property damage.  

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = attacktype1, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Attack Type",
    x = "Attack Type",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

The month of attack variable has pretty similar results across each month.  Some months such as month twelve or month four have a spike in no property damage, while most of the other months have equal amounts of yes property damage and no property damage.

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = imonth, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Month of Attack",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

The region variable shows that some regions have a large amount of both no property damage and yes property damage such as regions six, ten, and eleven.  While the other regions have very little amount of terrorist attacks as a whole such as month four or twelve.

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = region, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Region of Attack",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

## Hypothesis Testing:

The next step of exploratory data analysis used is the hypothesis test on each potential covariate and the dependent variable or property.  Since all the covariates are categorical variable types and property is a categorical variable type a chi squared test of independence can be used for all of them.   Each null hypothesis is that the potential covariate and the property variable are independent of each other.  Each alternative hypothesis is that the potential covariate and the property variable are not independent of each other.  The alpha value that will be accepted is 0.05.

Null Hypothesis: Multiple attacks and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Multiple attacks and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableM = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$multiple)
xkabledply(contableM, title="Contingency table for property and multiple within dataset")
chitestM = chisq.test(contableM)
chitestM
```

Null Hypothesis: Weapon type and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Weapon type and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableW = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$weaptype1)
xkabledply(contableW, title="Contingency table for property and weapon type within dataset")
chitestW = chisq.test(contableW)
chitestW
```

Null Hypothesis: Attack type and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Attack type and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableA = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$attacktype1)
xkabledply(contableA, title="Contingency table for property and attack type within dataset")
chitestA = chisq.test(contableA)
chitestA
```

Null Hypothesis: Month of attack and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Month of attack and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableMO = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$imonth)
xkabledply(contableMO, title="Contingency table for property and month of attack within dataset")
chitestMO = chisq.test(contableMO)
chitestMO
```

Null Hypothesis: Region of attack and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Region of attack and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableR = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$region)
xkabledply(contableR, title="Contingency table for property and region of attack within dataset")
chitestR = chisq.test(contableR)
chitestR
```
Each potential covariate being multiple, weapon type, attack type, region, month have p-values that are less than our set alpha value.  This indicates that for each chi squared test we can reject the null hypotheses, indicating that each potential covariate is not independent of property.

## Logistic Regression Model 1:

### glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1 + imonth + region + weaptype1, data = terror2019, family = binomial):

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(stats)
model1 = glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1 + imonth + region + weaptype1, data = terror2019, family = binomial)
summary(model1)
```

### Accuracy:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
probs = predict(model1, type = "response")
pred = ifelse(probs > 0.5, 1, 0)
table(Predicted = pred, Actual= terror2019$property)
mean(pred == terror2019$property)
```

### ROC and AUC:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(pROC)
probs = predict(model1, type = "response")
r = roc(terror2019$property, probs)
plot(r)
auc(r)
best <- coords(r, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"), best.method = "youden")
best
```

### Deviance:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
p1 = pchisq(8958.7 - 7069.4, df = 40, lower.tail = FALSE)
p1
```

The first logistic regression model includes all the variables that could be covariates.  Looking at the coefficients the multiple, attack type, month, and region variables have statistically significance or lower than 0.05 p-values.  Weapon type does not have any statistical significance so it can be removed in the next model.  The accuracy for this model is approximately seventy one percent.  The auc for this model is about seventy eight point five percent.  Thus, this model is looking like it has pretty good results.  However, the deviance for this model is exactly zero.  This represents that this model is a perfect fit, which is never truly the case.  So even though this looked like a good model, it is most likely due to over fitting the data which is causing it to be a perfect model.

## Logistic Regression Model 2:

### glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1 + imonth, data = terror2019, family = binomial)

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(stats)
model2 = glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1 + imonth, data = terror2019, family = binomial)
summary(model2)
```

### Accuracy:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
probs1 = predict(model2, type = "response")
pred1 = ifelse(probs1 > 0.5, 1, 0)
table(Predicted = pred1, Actual= terror2019$property)
mean(pred1 == terror2019$property)
```
### ROC and AUC

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(pROC)
probs1 = predict(model2, type = "response")
r = roc(terror2019$property, probs1)
plot(r)
auc(r)
best <- coords(r, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"), best.method = "youden")
best
```

### Deviance:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
p2 = pchisq(8958.7 - 7616.2, df = 20, lower.tail = FALSE)
p2
```

Through different testing of plugging and chugging different sets of independent variables, model two is the one that showed the best results while also not being overfitted with data.  The variables that were chosen to be left in as covariates include multiple, attack type, and month variables.  This produced a model that has an aic of 7658.2, which was lower than any other model that was tested on.  Model two has an accuracy of about sixty seven point two percent.  Model two???s auc is about seventy three percent.  While model two???s deviance is less than 0.05, but not exactly zero indicating that it is statistically better than the null and saturated models and that it is not overfitting the data. 

Looking at each covariate it can be seen that not every type within a covariate is statistically significant.  One example being that attack type three, four, five, six, seven and nine are statistically significant when compared to attack type one.  However, attack type two and eight are not statistically significant compared to attack type one.  Also, only month two, four, and twelve have statistical significance when compared to month one for this logistic regression model.

### Odds Ratio:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
exp(coef(model2))
```

Looking at the odds ratios of each coefficient it can be seen that some variables are above one, some below one, and some very close to one.  This indicates for variables like multiple when compared to its reference variable has a sixty six percent increase in odds of property damage happening.  While variables like month three when compared to its reference variable have a twelve percent decrease in odds of property damage happening.  

## Logistic Regression Model 3(Just for comparison):

### glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1, data = terror2019, family = binomial):

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(stats)
model3 = glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1, data = terror2019, family = binomial)
summary(model3)
```

### Accuracy:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
probs2 = predict(model3, type = "response")
pred2 = ifelse(probs2 > 0.5, 1, 0)
table(Predicted = pred2, Actual= terror2019$property)
mean(pred2 == terror2019$property)
```

### ROC and AUC:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(pROC)
probs2 = predict(model3, type = "response")
r = roc(terror2019$property, probs2)
plot(r)
auc(r)
best <- coords(r, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"), best.method = "youden")
best
```

### Deviance:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
p2 = pchisq(8958.7 - 7664.5, df = 11, lower.tail = FALSE)
p2
```

The inclusion of this third model is just to give a comparison of why model two was selected as the best model.  Model 2 has a better predictive accuracy than model 3.  Model 2 also has a lower aic than model 3 indicating it is a better model even with model 2 getting penalized for having more independent variables.  Model 2 also has a higher auc than model 3 indicating that model 2 is better at distinguishing the different cases than model 3.

## Conclusion:

To conclude, depending on the number of attacks within one terrorist attack, the type of terrorist attack, and the month in which the terrorist attack occurs can either increase or decrease the odds that your property will have damage after the terrorist attack.  Those three variables are the most influential variables on likelihood of property damage.  So, if for some reason a terrorist attack happened in 2019 and I happened to be there, I would hope that it would be one attack of type six which is a hostage taking or kidnapping, and is in April so that I would have the lowest odds of having damage to my car or house.



---
title: "War on Terror project 2 Ali Mutasim"
output: html_document
date: "2026-04-16"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r}

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(MASS)     
library(rpart)   
library(rpart.plot)
```

libraries were imported to do data manipulation, visualization, regression modeling, feature selection, and decsision trees. 


```{r}
Terror <- read.csv("TerrorData.csv")

Terror$iyear <- as.numeric(Terror$iyear)
Terror$nkill <- as.numeric(Terror$nkill)
Terror$nwound <- as.numeric(Terror$nwound)
Terror$propvalue <- as.numeric(Terror$propvalue)

Terror$nkill[is.na(Terror$nkill)] <- 0
Terror$nwound[is.na(Terror$nwound)] <- 0
Terror$propvalue[is.na(Terror$propvalue)] <- 0
```

the variables are converted into numeric form so that R can analzye and calculate well. it replaces NA values with zeros so that errors aren't caused.


```{r}
Terror$casualties <- Terror$nkill + Terror$nwound
Terror$log_casualties <- log1p(Terror$casualties)
Terror$log_prop <- log1p(Terror$propvalue)

Terror$period <- ifelse(Terror$iyear < 2001, "Pre-2001", "Post-2001")
Terror$period <- as.factor(Terror$period)
```
here number of killed and wounded into one variable called casualties. variable to show severity. log transforamtion is used to make them suitable for regression modeling. a variable period is created so that attacks are compared pre and post 2001.

```{r}
attacks_per_year <- Terror %>%
  group_by(iyear) %>%
  summarise(n_attacks = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(period = ifelse(iyear < 2001, "Pre-2001", "Post-2001"))

ggplot(attacks_per_year, aes(x = iyear, y = n_attacks)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2001, linetype="dashed")

ggplot(attacks_per_year, aes(x = period, y = n_attacks)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill="lightblue")

t.test(n_attacks ~ period, data = attacks_per_year)
```


Plots are created here by grouping attacks in each year and seeing how the frequency of these attaks change over time. a line plot is created to show how the number of attacks changes over time. a boxplot is created to show the frequency of attacks pre and post 2001 or 9/11


```{r}
# Scatterplot: casualties vs year
ggplot(Terror, aes(x = iyear, y = casualties)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

# Correlation matrix (numeric only)
numeric_vars <- Terror[, c("iyear","nkill","nwound","propvalue","casualties")]
cor(numeric_vars, use="complete.obs")

```
This step involves two-variable graphics to examine the relationships in the data. To determine whether the intensity of attacks varies with time a scatterplot of year vs casualties is plotted. The numeric variables are also correlated to one another, so that you can learn whether or not the variables move together, and whether there are any strong linear associations among them.


```{r}
model_full <- lm(log_casualties ~ iyear + attacktype1_txt + region_txt + period,
                 data = Terror)

summary(model_full)
```

A regression model is then constructed to describe the severity of attacks using the script. In this model, the dependent variable will be the log-transformed number of casualties, with the predictors being year, type of attack, region and period. This enables you to test the relationship between that and the other factors in the model and the other factors that are associated with more severe attacks.


```{r}
model_step <- stepAIC(model_full, direction = "both")

summary(model_step)
```
Once the entire regression model has been fitted, the script will select features by relying on stepAIC. The process eliminates variables which do not contribute to the model to the extent desired and retains the combination of predictors which offers the best tradeoff between model fit and simplicity. This is useful in creating a model that is less complicated and can be more efficient.


```{r}
# R-squared
summary(model_step)$r.squared

# Adjusted R-squared
summary(model_step)$adj.r.squared

# ANOVA comparison
anova(model_step)
```

After the last model has been selected, the script analyses the performance of the model. It analyzes the value of the R-squared and adjusted R-squared to understand the extent of the variation in the severity of the attack explained by the model. It also compares ANOVA output to determine the significance of the terms fitted in the model.

```{r}
par(mfrow=c(1,1))
plot(model_step)
```

Improper assumptions of the regression model are then verified using the regression model on the basis of diagnostic plots. These plots assist you to observe whether or not the residual values are approximately normal, the variance is reasonably constant, and whether there are influential points that may substantially affect the outcomes. This is significant in the sense that when the assumptions of the regression are moderately satisfied, the conclusions of the regression are more trustworthy.

```{r}
model_prop <- lm(log_prop ~ iyear + attacktype1_txt + region_txt + period,
                 data = Terror)

summary(model_prop)
```

The script then makes a second regression model with log-transformed property damage as a dependent variable. The reason behind this is that the severity that your project is gauging on is not only based on casualties, but also on property damages, which is the second outcome. This model assists in determining what variables are related to more harmful attacks in terms of property damage.

```{r}
tree_model <- rpart(log_casualties ~ iyear + attacktype1_txt + region_txt + period,
                    data = Terror,
                    method = "anova")

rpart.plot(tree_model)
```
A decision tree model of casualties is also provided in the script. A decision tree is more flexible in capturing nonlinear patterns and interactions than linear regression. This provides you another modeling method to contrast the regression results and makes your project stronger in the technical aspect.

```{r}
chisq.test(table(Terror$attacktype1_txt, Terror$period))
chisq.test(table(Terror$region_txt, Terror$period))

```

Lastly, chi-square tests are employed in the script to examine the change in categorical patterns with time. One chi-square test is used to test whether the distribution of attacks type differs between the pre-2001 and the post-2001 period, and another one to test whether the regional distribution of attacks varies across the period. These tests are useful in ascertaining whether patterns of terrorism changed in terms of structure following 9/11, and not merely in terms of frequency or intensity.
Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.



author of the section Pablo Dragnic

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE, fig.align = "center")
```

## Introduction

The Global Terrorism Database (GTD) records terrorist attacks worldwide from 1970 to 2020. This analysis focuses on **2019** and investigates what factors influence whether a terrorist attack is **successful or not**. According to the GTD codebook, the `success` variable is coded as `1` if the attack achieved its intended effect (e.g., bomb detonated, hostage taken, target killed), and `0` otherwise. This is distinct from strategic success ??? it measures tactical completion.

We examine the following predictor variables:

- **Attack type** (`attacktype1`)
- **Weapon type** (`weaptype1`)
- **Target type** (`targtype1`)
- **Region** (`region`)
- **Suicide attack** (`suicide`)
- **Part of multiple incident** (`multiple`)

We use EDA, chi-square tests, and logistic regression to answer the research question.

---

## 1. Data Loading and Preparation

## Variable Key:

#### Success:

0 = No (Attack was not tactically successful)

1 = Yes (Attack achieved its intended effect)

#### AttackType1:

1 = Assassination

2 = Armed Assault

3 = Bombing/Explosion

4 = Hijacking

5 = Hostage Taking (Barricade Incident)

6 = Hostage Taking (Kidnapping)

7 = Facility/Infrastructure Attack

8 = Unarmed Assault

9 = Unknown

#### WeaponType1:

1 = Biological

2 = Chemical

5 = Firearms

6 = Explosives

8 = Incendiary

9 = Melee

10 = Vehicle

11 = Sabotage Equipment

12 = Other

13 = Unknown

#### Region:

1 = North America

2 = Central America & Caribbean

3 = South America

4 = East Asia

5 = Southeast Asia

6 = South Asia

7 = Central Asia

8 = Western Europe

9 = Eastern Europe

10 = Middle East & North Africa

11 = Sub-Saharan Africa

12 = Australasia & Oceania

#### Suicide:

0 = No

1 = Yes

#### Multiple:

0 = No (Single incident)

1 = Yes (Part of coordinated multiple incident)

```{r load-data}
library(ggplot2)
library(ezids)
library(pROC)
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)

# Set working directory to wherever this .Rmd file lives (works for all team members)
setwd(dirname(rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path))

# Load dataset (already filtered to 2019)
gtd_full <- read.csv("TerrorData-2019.csv")
terror2019 <- gtd_full

# Select relevant columns
terror2019 <- terror2019[, c("success", "attacktype1", "weaptype1", "targtype1",
                              "region", "suicide", "multiple")]

# Convert to factors (numeric codes kept for models)
terror2019$success     <- as.factor(terror2019$success)
terror2019$attacktype1 <- as.factor(terror2019$attacktype1)
terror2019$weaptype1   <- as.factor(terror2019$weaptype1)
terror2019$targtype1   <- as.factor(terror2019$targtype1)
terror2019$region      <- as.factor(terror2019$region)
terror2019$suicide     <- as.factor(terror2019$suicide)
terror2019$multiple    <- as.factor(terror2019$multiple)

# Labeled versions for readable plots
attack_labels <- c("1"="Assassination","2"="Armed Assault","3"="Bombing/Explosion",
                   "4"="Hijacking","5"="Barricade Incident","6"="Kidnapping",
                   "7"="Facility Attack","8"="Unarmed Assault","9"="Unknown")
weapon_labels <- c("1"="Biological","2"="Chemical","5"="Firearms","6"="Explosives",
                   "8"="Incendiary","9"="Melee","10"="Vehicle","11"="Sabotage",
                   "12"="Other","13"="Unknown")
region_labels <- c("1"="North America","2"="Cent. America & Caribbean",
                   "3"="South America","4"="East Asia","5"="Southeast Asia",
                   "6"="South Asia","7"="Central Asia","8"="Western Europe",
                   "9"="Eastern Europe","10"="Middle East & N. Africa",
                   "11"="Sub-Saharan Africa","12"="Australasia & Oceania")
targ_labels   <- c("1"="Business","2"="Government (General)","3"="Police",
                   "4"="Military","5"="Abortion Related","6"="Airports & Aircraft",
                   "7"="Government (Diplomatic)","8"="Educational Institution",
                   "9"="Food/Water Supply","10"="Journalists & Media",
                   "11"="Maritime","12"="NGO","13"="Other",
                   "14"="Private Citizens","15"="Religious","16"="Telecom",
                   "17"="Terrorists/Militias","18"="Tourists",
                   "19"="Transportation","20"="Unknown","21"="Utilities",
                   "22"="Violent Political Parties")

terror2019$attack_lbl <- attack_labels[as.character(terror2019$attacktype1)]
terror2019$weapon_lbl <- weapon_labels[as.character(terror2019$weaptype1)]
terror2019$region_lbl <- region_labels[as.character(terror2019$region)]
terror2019$targ_lbl   <- targ_labels[as.character(terror2019$targtype1)]

str(terror2019)
```

The dataset is filtered to 2019 attacks only and narrowed to the seven variables of interest. All predictors and the outcome are converted to factors so that R treats them as categorical rather than continuous numeric values ??? this is important for logistic regression because it ensures each category gets its own dummy variable rather than being treated as an ordered scale. Human-readable label vectors are also merged onto the data frame for use in the plots, since the raw numeric codes from the GTD are not self-explanatory on their own.

---

## 2. Exploratory Data Analysis

### 2.1 Overall Success Rate

The first thing to examine is how balanced the outcome variable is. This matters because a heavily imbalanced outcome can make model accuracy misleading ??? a model that always predicts the majority class would look accurate without actually learning anything.

```{r overall-success}
total    <- nrow(terror2019)
n_succ   <- sum(terror2019$success == 1)
n_unsucc <- sum(terror2019$success == 0)

cat("Total attacks in 2019:", total, "\n")
cat("Successful attacks:", n_succ, "(", round(n_succ/total*100, 1), "%)\n")
cat("Unsuccessful attacks:", n_unsucc, "(", round(n_unsucc/total*100, 1), "%)\n")
```

In 2019, the vast majority of recorded attacks were successful. The `success` variable is imbalanced, which we will keep in mind when interpreting model results. Because there are far more successes than failures in the data, a naive model that always predicts "successful" would achieve high accuracy without any real discriminatory ability. This means we will rely on AUC, sensitivity, and specificity rather than accuracy alone when evaluating and comparing models.

```{r overall-plot}
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = success, fill = success)) +
  geom_bar(color = "black", width = 0.5) +
  geom_text(stat = "count", aes(label = ..count..), vjust = -0.5, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "#d9534f", "1" = "#5cb85c"),
                    labels = c("Unsuccessful", "Successful")) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("0" = "Unsuccessful", "1" = "Successful")) +
  labs(title = "Figure 1: Overall Attack Success in 2019",
       x = "Outcome", y = "Count", fill = "Outcome") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Figure 1 confirms the imbalance visually. The green bar for successful attacks is much larger than the red bar for unsuccessful ones. This pattern is consistent with how the GTD is constructed ??? attacks that are fully documented tend to be those that actually occurred and achieved some effect, while foiled attempts are harder to capture systematically in the data.

### 2.2 Success by Attack Type

```{r plot-attacktype}
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = reorder(attack_lbl, table(attack_lbl)[attack_lbl]),
                       fill = success)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "#d9534f", "1" = "#5cb85c"),
                    labels = c("Unsuccessful", "Successful")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Figure 2: Attack Success by Attack Type",
       x = "Attack Type", y = "Count", fill = "Success") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Figure 2 shows that Bombing/Explosion and Armed Assault account for the largest share of total attacks in 2019. Most attack types show a high ratio of successful outcomes, but there is meaningful variation. Hijacking and Barricade Incidents have a noticeably higher proportion of failures relative to other methods ??? these tactics require prolonged engagement and give security forces more time to respond and intervene. On the other end, Assassination and Facility Attacks show near-complete success rates, suggesting that once these methods are deployed they are very difficult to stop. This visual pattern strongly suggests attack type will be a meaningful predictor in the regression models.

### 2.3 Success by Weapon Type

```{r plot-weaptype}
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = reorder(weapon_lbl, table(weapon_lbl)[weapon_lbl]),
                       fill = success)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "#d9534f", "1" = "#5cb85c"),
                    labels = c("Unsuccessful", "Successful")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Figure 3: Attack Success by Weapon Type",
       x = "Weapon Type", y = "Count", fill = "Success") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Weapon type codes: 1=Biological, 2=Chemical, 5=Firearms, 6=Explosives, 8=Incendiary, 9=Melee, 10=Vehicle, 11=Sabotage, 12=Other, 13=Unknown.

Figure 3 shows that Firearms and Explosives dominate in raw count, consistent with the attack types seen above. Melee and Incendiary weapons show very high success rates ??? once an arson or close-quarters attack is underway it is extremely difficult to stop. Firearms show a slightly higher proportion of failures, possibly because armed confrontations give targets or law enforcement a chance to fight back. It is also worth noting that several weapon categories like Biological, Chemical, and Sabotage have very few observations in 2019. Model estimates for those rare categories will be unreliable and come with very wide confidence intervals.

### 2.4 Success by Region

```{r plot-region}
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = reorder(region_lbl, table(region_lbl)[region_lbl]),
                       fill = success)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "#d9534f", "1" = "#5cb85c"),
                    labels = c("Unsuccessful", "Successful")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Figure 4: Attack Success by Region",
       x = "Region", y = "Count", fill = "Success") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Figure 4 shows that South Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, and the Middle East & North Africa account for the bulk of attacks in 2019. These regions also show high absolute counts of successful attacks. Western Europe and North America have far fewer total incidents and show a visibly higher proportion of failures ??? consistent with having stronger counter-terrorism infrastructure and faster law enforcement response times. Regions like Central Asia and Australasia & Oceania contribute very few observations, so their regional estimates in the regression should be interpreted with caution.

### 2.4b Success by Target Type

```{r plot-targtype}
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = reorder(targ_lbl, table(targ_lbl)[targ_lbl]),
                       fill = success)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "#d9534f", "1" = "#5cb85c"),
                    labels = c("Unsuccessful", "Successful")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Figure 4b: Attack Success by Target Type",
       x = "Target Type", y = "Count", fill = "Success") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Figure 4b shows that Private Citizens, Military, Police, and Government (General) are the most frequently targeted groups. Military and Police targets have comparatively more unsuccessful attacks ??? these are hardened targets that can resist or respond. In contrast, Private Citizens and Religious targets show very high success rates, which reflects how vulnerable soft civilian targets tend to be. Several target categories like Maritime, Abortion Related, and Telecom appear very rarely in 2019, which will limit what the regression can reliably say about those specific categories.

### 2.5 Success by Suicide Attack

```{r plot-suicide}
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = suicide, fill = success)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "#d9534f", "1" = "#5cb85c"),
                    labels = c("Unsuccessful", "Successful")) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("0" = "Non-Suicide", "1" = "Suicide")) +
  labs(title = "Figure 5: Attack Success by Suicide Attack Status",
       x = "Suicide Attack", y = "Count", fill = "Success") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Figure 5 is one of the most striking visuals in the EDA. Suicide attacks, while far less common than non-suicide attacks in absolute numbers, are associated with a near-perfect success rate. The logic is straightforward: an attacker who has already committed to dying cannot be deterred by personal risk, arrested mid-operation, or convinced to abort. The proportion of unsuccessful non-suicide attacks is noticeably higher by comparison. This variable looks like it will be one of the strongest predictors in the logistic regression models.

### 2.6 Success by Multiple Incident

```{r plot-multiple}
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = multiple, fill = success)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "#d9534f", "1" = "#5cb85c"),
                    labels = c("Unsuccessful", "Successful")) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("0" = "Single", "1" = "Multiple")) +
  labs(title = "Figure 6: Attack Success by Part of Multiple Incident",
       x = "Part of Multiple Incident", y = "Count", fill = "Success") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Figure 6 shows that attacks which were part of a coordinated multiple-incident campaign have a slightly higher proportion of successes compared to standalone attacks. The difference is less dramatic than what we saw for suicide status, but it is still visible. Coordinated campaigns likely reflect better-organized, better-resourced groups with stronger operational planning ??? all factors that would naturally improve the odds of completing an attack.

---

## 3. Chi-Square Tests

For each predictor, we test whether it is independent of attack success. Alpha = 0.05 throughout. Since all the covariates and the outcome are categorical, the chi-square test of independence is the appropriate hypothesis test here. The null hypothesis for each test is that the predictor and success are independent. The alternative is that they are not independent. Rejecting the null indicates a statistically significant association and justifies including the variable in the logistic regression.

### 3.1 Attack Type vs. Success

**H0:** Attack type and success are independent.  
**H1:** Attack type and success are not independent.

```{r chi-attacktype}
contable_att <- table(terror2019$success, terror2019$attacktype1)
xkabledply(contable_att, title = "Contingency Table: Success vs. Attack Type")
chisq.test(contable_att)
```

The p-value is well below 0.05, so we **reject the null hypothesis**. Attack type and success are not independent. The contingency table shows the pattern clearly ??? Hijacking and Barricade Incidents have higher failure counts relative to their totals, while Assassination and Facility Attacks are almost uniformly successful. These differences are too large to be explained by random chance.

### 3.2 Weapon Type vs. Success

**H0:** Weapon type and success are independent.  
**H1:** Weapon type and success are not independent.

```{r chi-weaptype}
contable_wep <- table(terror2019$success, terror2019$weaptype1)
xkabledply(contable_wep, title = "Contingency Table: Success vs. Weapon Type")
chisq.test(contable_wep)
```

The p-value is again below 0.05, so we **reject the null hypothesis**. Weapon type is associated with attack success. Some cells in the contingency table have very low counts, which can make the chi-square approximation slightly less precise, but the overall association is still evident. Certain weapon types like Melee and Incendiary appear almost exclusively in successful attacks, while others show more variation.

### 3.3 Target Type vs. Success

**H0:** Target type and success are independent.  
**H1:** Target type and success are not independent.

```{r chi-targtype}
contable_targ <- table(terror2019$success, terror2019$targtype1)
xkabledply(contable_targ, title = "Contingency Table: Success vs. Target Type")
chisq.test(contable_targ)
```

Significant result (p < 0.05), so we **reject the null hypothesis**. Target type is associated with success. Hardened targets like Military and Police show more failures, while soft targets like Private Citizens show lower failure rates. This makes intuitive sense ??? a target that is armed and trained to respond will have a better chance of preventing an attack from completing than an unarmed civilian target.

### 3.4 Region vs. Success

**H0:** Region and success are independent.  
**H1:** Region and success are not independent.

```{r chi-region}
contable_reg <- table(terror2019$success, terror2019$region)
xkabledply(contable_reg, title = "Contingency Table: Success vs. Region")
chisq.test(contable_reg)
```

Region is significantly associated with success (p < 0.05), so we **reject the null hypothesis**. Where an attack takes place matters. Regions with active conflicts or weaker state institutions show more successful attacks, while high-capacity regions in Western Europe and North America see proportionally more disrupted attempts. The geographic variation is statistically real and not just noise.

### 3.5 Suicide vs. Success

**H0:** Suicide attack status and success are independent.  
**H1:** Suicide attack status and success are not independent.

```{r chi-suicide}
contable_sui <- table(terror2019$success, terror2019$suicide)
xkabledply(contable_sui, title = "Contingency Table: Success vs. Suicide")
chisq.test(contable_sui)
```

The p-value is below 0.05, confirming what Figure 5 showed visually ??? we **reject the null hypothesis**. The contingency table makes the pattern stark: the failure count for suicide attacks is extremely low, while non-suicide attacks show a meaningful number of unsuccessful outcomes. This is one of the clearest associations in the entire analysis.

### 3.6 Multiple Incident vs. Success

**H0:** Part of multiple incident and success are independent.  
**H1:** Part of multiple incident and success are not independent.

```{r chi-multiple}
contable_mul <- table(terror2019$success, terror2019$multiple)
xkabledply(contable_mul, title = "Contingency Table: Success vs. Multiple Incident")
chisq.test(contable_mul)
```

Significant result (p < 0.05), so we **reject the null hypothesis** here as well. Being part of a multiple incident is associated with higher success. All six predictors pass the chi-square screening, giving us statistical justification for including all of them as candidates in the logistic regression models.

---

## 4. Logistic Regression

Since `success` is a binary outcome, we use logistic regression (GLM with binomial family). We build and compare three models of increasing complexity. A helper function is defined to extract clean performance metrics from each model.

Because all predictors are categorical, R creates dummy variables for each level, with the lowest numeric level as the reference category. The coefficients represent log-odds, so we exponentiate them to get odds ratios (OR). An OR above 1 means that category increases the odds of success relative to the reference; an OR below 1 means it reduces the odds. A confidence interval that does not include 1 indicates a statistically significant effect.

```{r helper-functions}
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# Master label lookup: maps raw coefficient names -> readable labels
label_map <- c(
  "(Intercept)"    = "(Intercept)",
  # Attack type
  "attacktype12"   = "Attack: Armed Assault",
  "attacktype13"   = "Attack: Bombing/Explosion",
  "attacktype14"   = "Attack: Hijacking",
  "attacktype15"   = "Attack: Barricade Incident",
  "attacktype16"   = "Attack: Kidnapping",
  "attacktype17"   = "Attack: Facility Attack",
  "attacktype18"   = "Attack: Unarmed Assault",
  "attacktype19"   = "Attack: Unknown",
  # Weapon type
  "weaptype12"     = "Weapon: Chemical",
  "weaptype15"     = "Weapon: Firearms",
  "weaptype16"     = "Weapon: Explosives",
  "weaptype18"     = "Weapon: Incendiary",
  "weaptype19"     = "Weapon: Melee",
  "weaptype110"    = "Weapon: Vehicle",
  "weaptype111"    = "Weapon: Sabotage",
  "weaptype112"    = "Weapon: Other",
  "weaptype113"    = "Weapon: Unknown",
  # Target type
  "targtype12"     = "Target: Government (General)",
  "targtype13"     = "Target: Police",
  "targtype14"     = "Target: Military",
  "targtype15"     = "Target: Abortion Related",
  "targtype16"     = "Target: Airports & Aircraft",
  "targtype17"     = "Target: Government (Diplomatic)",
  "targtype18"     = "Target: Educational Institution",
  "targtype19"     = "Target: Food/Water Supply",
  "targtype110"    = "Target: Journalists & Media",
  "targtype111"    = "Target: Maritime",
  "targtype112"    = "Target: NGO",
  "targtype113"    = "Target: Other",
  "targtype114"    = "Target: Private Citizens",
  "targtype115"    = "Target: Religious",
  "targtype116"    = "Target: Telecom",
  "targtype117"    = "Target: Terrorists/Militias",
  "targtype118"    = "Target: Tourists",
  "targtype119"    = "Target: Transportation",
  "targtype120"    = "Target: Unknown",
  "targtype121"    = "Target: Utilities",
  "targtype122"    = "Target: Violent Political Parties",
  # Region
  "region2"        = "Region: Cent. America & Caribbean",
  "region3"        = "Region: South America",
  "region4"        = "Region: East Asia",
  "region5"        = "Region: Southeast Asia",
  "region6"        = "Region: South Asia",
  "region7"        = "Region: Central Asia",
  "region8"        = "Region: Western Europe",
  "region9"        = "Region: Eastern Europe",
  "region10"       = "Region: Middle East & N. Africa",
  "region11"       = "Region: Sub-Saharan Africa",
  "region12"       = "Region: Australasia & Oceania",
  # Suicide / multiple
  "suicide1"       = "Suicide Attack: Yes",
  "multiple1"      = "Part of Multiple Incident: Yes"
)

# Helper: formatted coefficient table with OR and 95% CI
coef_table <- function(model, title = "Coefficient Table") {
  s      <- summary(model)$coefficients
  or     <- exp(s[, 1])
  ci     <- exp(confint.default(model))
  df_out <- data.frame(
    Predictor    = ifelse(rownames(s) %in% names(label_map),
                          label_map[rownames(s)], rownames(s)),
    Coefficient  = round(s[, 1], 4),
    Std.Error    = round(s[, 2], 4),
    z.value      = round(s[, 3], 4),
    p.value      = round(s[, 4], 4),
    OR           = round(or, 4),
    CI_lower_95  = round(ci[, 1], 4),
    CI_upper_95  = round(ci[, 2], 4)
  )
  rownames(df_out) <- NULL
  kable(df_out, caption = title) %>%
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                  full_width = FALSE, font_size = 12)
}

# Helper: confusion matrix metrics table (handles incomplete matrices)
conf_metrics <- function(pred, actual, model_name = "Model") {
  cm   <- table(factor(pred, levels = c(0,1)),
                factor(actual, levels = c(0,1)))
  TN   <- cm[1, 1]; FP <- cm[2, 1]
  FN   <- cm[1, 2]; TP <- cm[2, 2]
  acc  <- round((TP + TN) / sum(cm), 4)
  sens <- round(TP  / (TP + FN), 4)
  spec <- round(TN  / (TN + FP), 4)
  prec <- ifelse((TP + FP) == 0, NA, round(TP / (TP + FP), 4))
  f1   <- ifelse(is.na(prec), NA, round(2 * prec * sens / (prec + sens), 4))
  data.frame(Model       = model_name,
             Accuracy    = acc,
             Sensitivity = sens,
             Specificity = spec,
             Precision   = prec,
             F1_Score    = f1)
}
```

### 4.1 Model 1: Full Model (All Predictors)

Model 1 includes all six predictors: attack type, weapon type, target type, region, suicide status, and multiple incident. This is the most complex model and serves as an upper bound ??? if a simpler model achieves similar performance, we have good reason to prefer it on parsimony grounds.

```{r model1}
model1 <- glm(success ~ attacktype1 + weaptype1 + targtype1 + region + suicide + multiple,
              data = terror2019, family = binomial)
```

**Table 8: Coefficients, Odds Ratios, and 95% CI  Model 1**

```{r model1-coeftable, results='asis'}
coef_table(model1, title = "Table 8: Model 1  Coefficients, OR, and 95% CI")
```

Looking at the coefficients in Table 8, the suicide attack variable has one of the largest positive effects in the full model, confirming the strong visual pattern from the EDA. Several attack type categories are statistically significant compared to the Assassination reference, particularly Hijacking and Barricade Incident which show reduced odds of success. Interestingly, not all weapon type or target type coefficients are significant once all variables are controlled for simultaneously ??? this is a sign that some of the bivariate associations seen in the EDA were partly explained by overlap with other predictors.

**Confusion Matrix and Performance Metrics  Model 1:**

```{r model1-confusion}
probs1 <- predict(model1, type = "response")
pred1  <- ifelse(probs1 > 0.5, 1, 0)
cm1    <- table(Predicted = pred1, Actual = terror2019$success)
xkabledply(cm1, title = "Confusion Matrix  Model 1")
metrics1 <- conf_metrics(pred1, terror2019$success, "Model 1 (Full)")
kable(metrics1, caption = "Table 9: Performance Metrics  Model 1") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

The confusion matrix shows that Model 1 correctly classifies most successful attacks (high sensitivity) but struggles more with the minority class of unsuccessful attacks (lower specificity). This is expected given the class imbalance ??? the model has far more examples of successful attacks to learn from. Accuracy alone looks high on paper, but given the imbalance, AUC is the more informative metric here.

**ROC Curve and AUC  Model 1:**

```{r model1-roc}
roc1  <- roc(terror2019$success, probs1)
plot(roc1, main = "Figure 7: ROC Curve  Model 1 (Full Model)")
best1 <- coords(roc1, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"),
                best.method = "youden")
kable(as.data.frame(best1), digits = 4,
      caption = "Table 10: Best Threshold (Youden)  Model 1") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
cat("AUC Model 1:", round(auc(roc1), 4), "\n")
```

The AUC for Model 1 tells us how well the model distinguishes between successful and unsuccessful attacks across all possible thresholds. An AUC of 0.5 would be no better than a coin flip. The Youden-optimal threshold in Table 10 is the cut-point that maximizes the combined sensitivity and specificity ??? it is often lower than 0.5 when the outcome is imbalanced, which adjusts for the fact that unsuccessful attacks are much rarer in the data.

---

### 4.2 Model 2: Reduced Model (Attack Type + Weapon Type + Region + Suicide)

Model 2 drops target type and multiple incident from the full model. The reasoning is parsimony: these two variables showed weaker visual signals in the EDA compared to the others, and a model with fewer predictors is generally easier to interpret and less likely to overfit. The likelihood ratio test in Section 5 will formally confirm whether the reduced model loses significant explanatory power.

```{r model2}
model2 <- glm(success ~ attacktype1 + weaptype1 + region + suicide,
              data = terror2019, family = binomial)
```

**Table 11: Coefficients, Odds Ratios, and 95% CI  Model 2**

```{r model2-coeftable, results='asis'}
coef_table(model2, title = "Table 11: Model 2  Coefficients, OR, and 95% CI")
```

The coefficient pattern in Model 2 is broadly consistent with Model 1, which is reassuring ??? it means the estimates are stable and not being heavily distorted by the dropped variables. Suicide attack remains the strongest predictor. Attack types requiring prolonged standoffs continue to show reduced odds of success relative to Assassination. Region coefficients reflect the geographic variation from the EDA, with conflict-heavy regions showing higher odds of success and high-capacity regions showing lower odds compared to North America as the reference.

**Confusion Matrix and Performance Metrics  Model 2:**

```{r model2-confusion}
probs2 <- predict(model2, type = "response")
pred2  <- ifelse(probs2 > 0.5, 1, 0)
cm2    <- table(Predicted = pred2, Actual = terror2019$success)
xkabledply(cm2, title = "Confusion Matrix  Model 2")
metrics2 <- conf_metrics(pred2, terror2019$success, "Model 2 (Reduced)")
kable(metrics2, caption = "Table 12: Performance Metrics Model 2") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

**ROC Curve and AUC Model 2:**

```{r model2-roc}
roc2  <- roc(terror2019$success, probs2)
plot(roc2, main = "Figure 8: ROC Curve Model 2 (Reduced Model)")
best2 <- coords(roc2, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"),
                best.method = "youden")
kable(as.data.frame(best2), digits = 4,
      caption = "Table 13: Best Threshold (Youden) Model 2") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
cat("AUC Model 2:", round(auc(roc2), 4), "\n")
```

Model 2 achieves an AUC very close to Model 1, which suggests that removing target type and multiple incident did not meaningfully hurt the model's ability to distinguish between successful and unsuccessful attacks. If the likelihood ratio test confirms that the fit difference between Models 1 and 2 is not statistically significant, Model 2 would be the preferred choice ??? similar performance with fewer parameters and a lower risk of overfitting.

---

### 4.3 Model 3: Minimal Model (Attack Type + Suicide)

Model 3 reduces the specification to just the two variables with the clearest and most theoretically grounded associations with success throughout the analysis: attack type and suicide status. This serves as a useful lower bound. If this minimal model performs nearly as well as the others, it would suggest that most of the explainable variation in attack success can be captured by just these two variables.

```{r model3}
model3 <- glm(success ~ attacktype1 + suicide,
              data = terror2019, family = binomial)
```

**Table 14: Coefficients, Odds Ratios, and 95% CI Model 3**

```{r model3-coeftable, results='asis'}
coef_table(model3, title = "Table 14: Model 3 Coefficients, OR, and 95% CI")
```

With only two predictors, the coefficient estimates in Model 3 are similar in direction and sign to the more complex models. This consistency across all three model specifications is reassuring ??? it means the key relationships are real and not artifacts of a particular set of control variables. Suicide attack continues to show a large positive coefficient, and the attack type contrasts remain interpretable and directionally consistent.

**Confusion Matrix and Performance Metrics Model 3:**

```{r model3-confusion}
probs3 <- predict(model3, type = "response")
pred3  <- ifelse(probs3 > 0.5, 1, 0)
cm3    <- table(Predicted = pred3, Actual = terror2019$success)
xkabledply(cm3, title = "Confusion Matrix Model 3")
metrics3 <- conf_metrics(pred3, terror2019$success, "Model 3 (Minimal)")
kable(metrics3, caption = "Table 15: Performance Metrics Model 3") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

**ROC Curve and AUC Model 3:**

```{r model3-roc}
roc3  <- roc(terror2019$success, probs3)
plot(roc3, main = "Figure 9: ROC Curve Model 3 (Minimal Model)")
best3 <- coords(roc3, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"),
                best.method = "youden")
kable(as.data.frame(best3), digits = 4,
      caption = "Table 16: Best Threshold (Youden) Model 3") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
cat("AUC Model 3:", round(auc(roc3), 4), "\n")
```

---

## 5. Model Comparison

### 5.1 Likelihood Ratio Tests

The likelihood ratio test compares nested models by looking at the difference in their deviances. If the p-value is below 0.05, the extra parameters in the larger model genuinely improve fit beyond what chance would produce and are worth keeping. If the p-value is above 0.05, the simpler model is adequate and should be preferred.

```{r model-comparison}
null_dev <- model1$null.deviance
null_df  <- model1$df.null

p_m1    <- pchisq(null_dev - model1$deviance,
                  df = null_df - model1$df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE)
p_m2    <- pchisq(null_dev - model2$deviance,
                  df = null_df - model2$df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE)
p_m3    <- pchisq(null_dev - model3$deviance,
                  df = null_df - model3$df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE)
p_m1_m2 <- pchisq(model2$deviance - model1$deviance,
                   df = model2$df.residual - model1$df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE)
p_m2_m3 <- pchisq(model3$deviance - model2$deviance,
                   df = model3$df.residual - model2$df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE)

lrt_table <- data.frame(
  Comparison         = c("Model 1 vs Null", "Model 2 vs Null", "Model 3 vs Null",
                          "Model 1 vs Model 2", "Model 2 vs Model 3"),
  Chi_sq_statistic   = round(c(null_dev - model1$deviance,
                                null_dev - model2$deviance,
                                null_dev - model3$deviance,
                                model2$deviance - model1$deviance,
                                model3$deviance - model2$deviance), 2),
  df                 = c(null_df - model1$df.residual,
                          null_df - model2$df.residual,
                          null_df - model3$df.residual,
                          model2$df.residual - model1$df.residual,
                          model3$df.residual - model2$df.residual),
  p_value            = round(c(p_m1, p_m2, p_m3, p_m1_m2, p_m2_m3), 6)
)

kable(lrt_table, caption = "Table 17: Likelihood Ratio Tests Model Comparisons") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

All three models improve significantly over the null (intercept-only) model, confirming that the included predictors collectively explain real variation in attack success. The critical comparison is Model 1 vs. Model 2: if that p-value is above 0.05, it means target type and multiple incident do not add significant explanatory power once attack type, weapon type, region, and suicide are already in the model, and the simpler Model 2 is preferred. The Model 2 vs. Model 3 comparison tells us whether region and weapon type contribute meaningful improvement beyond attack type and suicide status alone.

### 5.2 Overall Model Performance Summary

```{r model-summary-table}
all_metrics <- rbind(metrics1, metrics2, metrics3)
all_metrics$AUC <- round(c(as.numeric(auc(roc1)),
                            as.numeric(auc(roc2)),
                            as.numeric(auc(roc3))), 4)
all_metrics$AIC <- round(c(AIC(model1), AIC(model2), AIC(model3)), 2)
all_metrics$Null_Deviance  <- round(c(model1$null.deviance,
                                       model2$null.deviance,
                                       model3$null.deviance), 2)
all_metrics$Resid_Deviance <- round(c(model1$deviance,
                                       model2$deviance,
                                       model3$deviance), 2)

kable(all_metrics,
      caption = "Table 18: Full Model Comparison Accuracy, AUC, AIC, Deviance") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  column_spec(2:8, bold = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which.max(all_metrics$AUC), bold = TRUE, color = "white", background = "#5cb85c")
```

The highlighted row indicates the best-performing model by AUC.

Table 18 puts all three models side by side across every key metric. Lower AIC is better ??? a model gets penalized for each additional parameter, so a lower AIC means the extra complexity was actually worth it. Higher AUC is better for discrimination. If Model 2 has a lower AIC than Model 1 and a competitive AUC, it is the better overall choice. Model 3 gives us a useful reference: if it falls noticeably short on AUC and AIC compared to Model 2, it confirms that region and weapon type are genuinely adding explanatory value and should not be dropped.

---

## 6. Odds Ratios Best Model

Based on AUC, AIC, and the likelihood ratio tests, we interpret odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals for the best-performing model (Model 2).

```{r odds-ratios}
or_vals <- exp(coef(model2))
ci_vals <- exp(confint.default(model2))

or_df <- data.frame(
  Predictor   = ifelse(names(or_vals) %in% names(label_map),
                       label_map[names(or_vals)], names(or_vals)),
  OR          = round(or_vals, 4),
  CI_lower_95 = round(ci_vals[, 1], 4),
  CI_upper_95 = round(ci_vals[, 2], 4)
)
rownames(or_df) <- NULL

kable(or_df, caption = "Table 19: Odds Ratios with 95% CI Model 2 (Best Model)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which(or_df$OR > 1 & or_df$CI_lower_95 > 1), background = "#d4edda") %>%
  row_spec(which(or_df$OR < 1 & or_df$CI_upper_95 < 1), background = "#f8d7da")
```

Rows highlighted in **green** indicate predictors that significantly *increase* the odds of success (OR > 1, CI entirely above 1). Rows in **red** indicate predictors that significantly *decrease* the odds of success.

Looking at the odds ratios in Table 19, a few findings stand out. The suicide attack variable has one of the largest positive ORs in the model ??? a suicide attack has substantially higher odds of succeeding compared to a non-suicide attack, and that confidence interval does not include 1, making it statistically significant. Among attack types, categories like Hijacking and Barricade Incident show ORs below 1 relative to the Assassination baseline, confirming these methods are more likely to fail once you control for weapon type, region, and suicide status. Several region coefficients are also significant, with conflict-heavy regions showing higher odds of success compared to North America. For weapon type, the effects are more mixed ??? some categories show meaningful ORs while others have wide confidence intervals reflecting the low cell counts we saw in the EDA for rare weapon types. Predictors whose confidence intervals cross 1 are not statistically significant at the 0.05 level and should not be interpreted as having a reliable directional effect.

---

## 7. Conclusions

Based on the 2019 GTD data (`r nrow(terror2019)` attacks), the chi-square tests indicate that **all six predictors** ??? attack type, weapon type, target type, region, suicide status, and whether the attack was part of a multiple incident ??? are statistically associated with attack success (all p-values < 0.05).

The logistic regression models confirm that **attack type** and **suicide status** are the strongest and most consistent predictors of success across all three model specifications. Suicide attacks are significantly more likely to succeed than non-suicide attacks ??? the attacker's commitment to dying in the attempt removes nearly all possibility of deterrence or disruption once underway. Among attack types, methods requiring prolonged engagement like Hijacking and Barricade Incidents have the highest failure rates, while Assassination and Facility Attacks are nearly always completed. Region and weapon type add meaningful predictive power on top of these two variables, while target type and multiple incident contribute less once the other factors are controlled for.

Model 2 ??? using attack type, weapon type, region, and suicide ??? offers the best balance of fit and parsimony, with competitive AUC and lower AIC relative to the full model. Thinking practically: if a terrorist attack occurred in 2019, the single biggest factor in whether it succeeded was whether the attacker was willing to die. Beyond that, the method of attack and where it happened mattered more than what kind of target was chosen or what weapon was used.
